success_surface: Correct multi-row formula creation with format preservation after iterative inspection
effective_behaviors: Iterative code inspection with error recovery - attempt inspection, handle API errors, correct and re-run before main logic
Parse existing formulas to extract dynamic parameters rather than hardcoding values
Verify structural conditions (merged cells, empty states) before executing conditional instructions
Capture formatting metadata (column widths, row heights) before modifications and restore afterward
Create backup copy before any write operations to preserve original state
Validate target data ranges against source worksheet content to confirm instruction alignment
Write Excel formulas as raw string literals to maintain calculation functionality
Use explicit sheet name access after programmatically listing available sheets
evidence: Initial inspection raised AttributeError on merge_cells; agent corrected code and re-inspected successfully
Extracted years from H column COUNTIF formulas using regex pattern matching on formula strings
Checked ws.merged_cells.ranges for I2:I11 even though instruction said 'if necessary'
Captured column_widths dict before loop, then restored all widths in second pass
Used shutil.copy() to create backup file before loading workbook for modification
Verified G14:G185 and J14:J185 contained expected year and resolution data via sampling
Set cell.value = f'=COUNTIFS(...)' to inject formula as string, not calculated result
Called wb.sheetnames first, then accessed ws = wb['Cumulative'] instead of wb.active

memory_items.title: Defensive Inspection Pattern
memory_items.lesson: When inspecting spreadsheets, expect potential API edge cases and validate cell/merge structures before manipulation
memory_items.generalization: Always include error recovery for inspection code; verify structural preconditions (merges, empty cells) match expectations before executing conditional actions

memory_items.title: Dynamic Parameter Extraction
memory_items.lesson: Derive formula parameters from existing workbook content rather than assuming constants
memory_items.generalization: For multi-row batch operations, scan relevant reference columns/formulas to build parameter lists dynamically for iteration

memory_items.title: Formatting Preservation Protocol
memory_items.lesson: Capture styling metadata before modifications and explicitly restore after changes
memory_items.generalization: Store column widths, row heights, and other dimension properties before editing; restore them post-modification to maintain visual consistency

memory_items.title: Safe Write Workflow
memory_items.lesson: Protect original data before any mutation through backup or separate output path
memory_items.generalization: Create a backup copy of the source file before loading for edits, ensuring recoverability if the operation fails or corrupts data

memory_items.title: Formula Injection Technique
memory_items.lesson: Write Excel formulas as raw strings to preserve calculation logic across reloads
memory_items.generalization: Always assign formulas as prefixed strings (e.g., '=SUM(...)') when using openpyxl; avoid storing evaluated results unless explicitly required

memory_items.title: Sheet Access Validation
memory_items.lesson: Confirm available sheet names before direct index-based access
memory_items.generalization: List sheet names programmatically before accessing by name or index; prefer explicit named references over .active for reliability

edits.content:
## 6. Trace-Derived Lessons

### Defensive Inspection
- Expect inspection API calls to fail on edge cases; implement try/except blocks around sheet merging checks and cell queries.
- Always verify preconditions (merged cells, empty ranges) before executing conditional instructions like "unmerge if needed".

### Dynamic Parameter Derivation
- For batch formula generation across multiple rows, scan existing formulas or reference columns to extract iteration parameters dynamically.
- Use regex or formula-parsing logic on existing cells to build parameter lists rather than hardcoding values.

### Formatting Preservation Protocol
- Capture all column widths and row heights into a dictionary before any modifications begin.
- After writing changes, iterate through stored dimensions and restore each one explicitly.
- This applies even when only formula cells change; adjacent formatting may shift otherwise.

### Safe Write Workflow
- Create a backup copy of the source file using shutil or equivalent before loading for modification.
- Save final output to a distinct path to enable comparison and recovery.
- Document what was backed up and where the modified version was written.

### Formula Injection Best Practices
- Always write Excel formulas as raw strings starting with '=' prefix.
- Validate that formulas reference valid cell ranges that exist in the actual data region.
- When generating formulas programmatically, use Python f-strings or format() methods safely to embed variable values.

### Sheet Access Reliability
- Query wb.sheetnames before accessing any sheet by name.
- Prefer explicit named sheet access (wb['SheetName']) over wb.active for consistent behavior.
- Log discovered sheet names during initial inspection for debugging clarity.
